section.title: God Is Known in Judah
section.sectionRationale: A victory hymn of Asaph celebrating God's dwelling in Zion and His shattering of enemy weapons, likely tied to a sudden deliverance of Jerusalem, this psalm shows the God known in Judah as fearsome to nations yet a savior of the lowly.
section.thesis: The God who dwells in Zion is known by shattering the weapons of war and stilling the mightiest warriors with a single rebuke, so that every ruler on earth owes Him fear, vows, and tribute, for He alone saves the lowly and humbles the proud.
section.frameNote: Four movements: God known and near in Judah, verses 1 to 3; weapons silenced and warriors stilled, verses 4 to 6; fearsome judgment that saves the lowly, verses 7 to 9; a call to loyal worship and tribute, verses 10 to 12.

units.label: God known and near in Judah

verses.text: For the choirmaster. With stringed instruments. A Psalm of Asaph. A song. God is known in Judah; His name is great in Israel.
His tent is in {{salem|Salem}}, His dwelling place in Zion.
There He shattered the flaming arrows, the shield and sword and weapons of war. Selah
You are resplendent with light, more majestic than mountains filled with game.
The valiant lie plundered; they sleep their last sleep. No men of might could lift a hand.
At Your rebuke, O God of Jacob, both horse and rider lie stunned.

units.label: Fearsome judgment that saves the lowly
units.note: the earth feared and was still

verses.text: You alone are to be {{yare|feared}}. When You are angry, who can stand before You?
From heaven You pronounced judgment, and the earth feared and was still
when God rose up to judge, to save all the lowly of the earth. Selah

units.label: A call to loyal worship and tribute
units.note: He breaks the spirits of princes

verses.text: Even the wrath of man shall praise You; with the survivors of wrath You will clothe Yourself.
Make and fulfill your vows to the LORD your God; let all the neighboring lands bring tribute to Him who is to be feared.
He breaks the spirits of princes; He is feared by the kings of the earth.

salem.gloss: Salem, an ancient name for Jerusalem meaning whole, complete, or peace, the same root behind shalom. God's tent pitched in a place whose very name means peace shows His dwelling as the source of true and lasting peace.

yare.gloss: To fear, to hold in reverent awe. This is not mere terror but the fitting response of creatures before their Creator and Judge, a fear that in this psalm leads not to despair but to worship, vows, and tribute.

basic.context: A victory hymn of Asaph celebrating that God, dwelling in Zion, shattered enemy weapons and stilled an entire army with a single rebuke, likely commemorating a sudden, dramatic deliverance of Jerusalem from a besieging force.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm celebrates a specific act of God's deliverance while declaring an abiding truth: the God who dwells with His people is to be feared by every earthly power, and that same fearsome judgment is what saves the lowly.
basic.originalLanguages: God's dwelling in Salem, shalem, whose very name means peace or wholeness, verse 2, shows His presence as the true source of peace, while the psalm insists He alone is to be yare, feared, verse 7, a reverent awe that leads to worship rather than despair.
basic.typology: God's dwelling in Salem, the ancient name for Jerusalem also tied to Melchizedek, king of Salem, Genesis 14:18, anticipates Christ, our great High Priest after the order of Melchizedek, Hebrews 7:1-2, the true King of peace whose reign shatters every weapon of war.
basic.christ: The God who shatters weapons of war and rises to judge yet save the lowly anticipates Christ, the Prince of Peace, Isaiah 9:6, whose cross disarmed the rulers and authorities, Colossians 2:15, winning the ultimate and only lasting peace this psalm's Salem, city of peace, points toward.
basic.god: God is shown as both fearsome, unmatched in majesty and power over armies and rulers, and merciful, rising specifically to save the lowly of the earth, a judgment that terrifies the proud yet rescues the humble.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 7:1-2 ties Salem to Melchizedek, a type of Christ as King of peace and righteousness. Colossians 2:15 shows Christ disarming rulers and authorities, the ultimate shattering of every weapon raised against God. Luke 1:52 echoes this psalm's pattern of God humbling the proud and lifting the lowly.
basic.oneStory: This celebration of God's fearsome, peace-bringing power in Zion threads into the whole Bible's story of a King who dwells among His people and shatters every weapon raised against them, fulfilled in Christ, the true Prince of Peace who disarmed every hostile power at the cross.

context.literary: A hymn built in three movements marked by two Selah pauses, verses 3 and 9, moving from local victory in Zion to a universal courtroom scene of heavenly judgment, and closing with a call to vows and tribute owed to the fearsome, saving King.
context.historical: Many connect this psalm to 701 BC, when the Angel of the LORD struck down the besieging Assyrian army around Jerusalem overnight, 2 Kings 19:35, Isaiah 37:36, an event matching the psalm's picture of warriors and horses suddenly stilled without a human hand raised.
context.canonical: Continuing Book III's themes of God's dwelling and defense of Zion, this psalm complements Psalm 46's 'God is our refuge and strength' and Psalm 48's celebration of Zion as the city of the great King.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To celebrate a specific, dramatic deliverance of Jerusalem as proof that God alone is to be feared by every earthly power, and to call all peoples to fitting worship, vows, and tribute in response.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes one historical act of deliverance in vivid, celebratory language; it prescribes as an abiding pattern that God's people should fear Him rightly, keep their vows, and trust Him as the one who both judges the proud and saves the lowly.

debates.question: Does verse 10, 'even the wrath of man shall praise You,' mean that human anger itself somehow honors God, or that God overrules human wrath for His own praise?

views.label: God directly turns human wrath into an occasion for His own praise and glory
views.case: The verse's parallel line, 'with the survivors of wrath You will clothe Yourself,' suggests God takes even the aftermath of human anger and wears it, so to speak, as a display of His sovereignty.

views.label: God simply restrains and limits human wrath so that only what serves His purposes is permitted to occur
views.case: An alternate rendering of the verse, reflected in some translations, is 'the remainder of wrath You will restrain,' suggesting the emphasis falls on God's limiting rather than repurposing human anger.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the same underlying truth: no human anger or hostility, however fierce, ultimately escapes God's sovereign control, and it will in the end serve to display His glory rather than thwart His purposes.

study.originalLanguages: God's tent is pitched in Salem, shalem, whose very name means peace or wholeness, verse 2, and the psalm insists He alone is yare, to be feared, verse 7, a reverent awe that in this psalm leads to worship and tribute rather than despair.

typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ as King of Salem, the true Melchizedek, our great High Priest and King of peace and righteousness, Hebrews 7:1-2.
Anticipates Christ's disarming of every hostile ruler and authority at the cross, Colossians 2:15.

study.christ: God's dwelling in Salem, an ancient name of Jerusalem tied to Melchizedek, 'king of Salem,' Genesis 14:18, anticipates Christ, our great High Priest after the order of Melchizedek, King of peace and righteousness, Hebrews 7:1-2. The shattering of enemy weapons in this psalm foreshadows Christ's own decisive victory, disarming the rulers and authorities and triumphing over them at the cross, Colossians 2:15, the true and final peace this psalm's city of Salem points toward.
study.god: God is fearsome beyond every earthly army, silencing horse and rider with a single rebuke, yet His fearsome judgment is aimed at saving the lowly of the earth, showing that His terror toward the proud and His mercy toward the humble are two sides of the same righteous character.

crossReferences.note: Ties Salem to Melchizedek, a type of Christ as King of peace and righteousness.
Shows Christ disarming every ruler and authority, the ultimate fulfillment of this psalm's shattered weapons.
Echoes this psalm's pattern that God rises to humble the proud and save the lowly.

study.oneStory: This victory hymn's picture of God dwelling in the city of peace and shattering every weapon raised against His people threads into the whole Bible's story of a King who disarms hostile powers, fulfilled in Christ, the true Prince of Peace and King of Salem, who triumphed over every ruler and authority at the cross.

quiz.q: Where is God's dwelling place, according to verses 1-2?

options.text: His tent is in Salem, His dwelling place in Zion.
options.note: This grounds the psalm's celebration in God's own chosen dwelling among His people.

options.text: In a temple built in Egypt.
options.note: The text names Salem and Zion, not Egypt.

options.text: Nowhere in particular; God is only in heaven.
options.note: The psalm specifically emphasizes God's dwelling among His people in Salem and Zion, not only a distant heaven.

options.text: In the wilderness, far from any city.
options.note: The text is explicit that His dwelling is in Salem and Zion, a city, not the wilderness.

quiz.q: What did God shatter there, according to verse 3?

options.text: The flaming arrows, shield, sword, and weapons of war.
options.note: This is the psalm's central picture of God's decisive victory over armed enemies.

options.text: The mountains surrounding Jerusalem.
options.note: The text specifies weapons of war, not mountains.

options.text: The idols of neighboring nations.
options.note: While God does oppose idolatry elsewhere, this verse specifically names weapons of war.

options.text: The city walls of Jerusalem itself.
options.note: The text describes enemy weapons being shattered, not the city's own walls.

quiz.q: What happens to the enemy army at God's rebuke, verses 5-6?

options.text: The valiant lie plundered and both horse and rider are stunned, unable to lift a hand.
options.note: A single rebuke from God renders the entire army powerless without a human hand raised against them.

options.text: They surrender peacefully after negotiations.
options.note: No negotiation is described; the army is supernaturally stilled by God's rebuke.

options.text: They retreat after a long siege.
options.note: The text describes sudden, supernatural stillness, not a prolonged siege and retreat.

options.text: They convert and join Israel's army.
options.note: No such conversion is mentioned; the army is simply rendered powerless.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of God's rising up to judge, according to verse 9?

options.text: To save all the lowly of the earth.
options.note: God's fearsome judgment is explicitly aimed at rescuing the humble and oppressed.

options.text: To destroy the whole earth without exception.
options.note: The text specifically names saving the lowly as the purpose, not total destruction.

options.text: To reward only the wealthy and powerful.
options.note: The opposite; God's judgment specifically favors the lowly, not the powerful.

options.text: To establish a new covenant with foreign kings.
options.note: The text describes judgment aimed at saving the lowly, not establishing covenants with foreign rulers.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's picture of God dwelling in Salem connect to the New Testament?

options.text: Salem is tied to Melchizedek, a type of Christ as our great High Priest and King of peace, Hebrews 7:1-2.
options.note: This is a direct typological connection drawn out explicitly in Hebrews.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of Jerusalem's future rebuilding.
options.note: The psalm makes no such specific prediction.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: Its Salem imagery is directly picked up in Hebrews 7 regarding Christ as the true Melchizedek.

options.text: It teaches that God no longer dwells among His people.
options.note: The opposite; the psalm and its fulfillment in Christ emphasize God's ongoing dwelling with His people.

questions: This psalm celebrates God's dwelling among His people in Salem, whose name means peace, verse 2. Where do you most need to experience God's peace in the middle of conflict right now?
God stills an entire army with a single rebuke, without a human hand raised, verses 5-6. What battle in your life do you need to entrust entirely to God rather than fighting in your own strength?

God's fearsome judgment is aimed specifically at saving the lowly of the earth, verse 9. Where do you need to trust that God's justice is ultimately good news for the humble, even when it feels frightening?
The psalm calls worshipers to make and fulfill their vows to the Lord, verse 11. What vow or commitment to God do you need to renew or finally keep?

Salem points forward to Christ, our true King of peace who disarmed every hostile power at the cross, Colossians 2:15. What hostile power, whether fear, sin, or circumstance, do you need to remember Christ has already disarmed?

translationNotes.note: 'Salem' is an ancient, shorter name for Jerusalem, also used in Genesis 14:18 of Melchizedek, king of Salem, and built from the same Hebrew root as shalom, peace.

The second half of this verse is difficult in Hebrew and can also be rendered 'Surely Your wrath against men brings You praise, and the survivors of Your wrath will be restrained,' shifting the emphasis from God repurposing wrath to God limiting it.

No em or en dashes present. No sermon from the trusted cluster could be confirmed on Psalm 76 this session, so status is 'none' rather than risk fabrication.
